questions.question: What is the primary mechanical difference between executing `git merge main` and `git rebase main` on a divergent feature branch?
options: Merge deletes your branch, Rebase keeps it globally

Merge creates a brand new 'Merge Commit' to permanently tie two divergent timelines together. Rebase mathematically unplugs your commits, fast-forwards the base, and replays your commits linearly on top of the newest code, completely eliminating the need for a Merge Commit
Rebase only works natively on Linux environments
Merge resolves conflicts automatically, Rebase mathematically prevents conflicts
questions.answer: Merge creates a brand new 'Merge Commit' to permanently tie two divergent timelines together. Rebase mathematically unplugs your commits, fast-forwards the base, and replays your commits linearly on top of the newest code, completely eliminating the need for a Merge Commit
questions.explanation: If 10 developers merge daily, the history log looks incredibly messy and entangled. By rebasing, everyone's work appears to have been mathematically coded in a perfectly straight, linear chronological line.

questions.question: What highly requested capability does executing an 'Interactive Rebase' (`git rebase -i HEAD~5`) provide a developer before they push to a central repository?
options: It provides a GUI visualization of the repository tree globally

It temporarily halts the timeline and opens an interactive script allowing the developer to alter history natively; enabling them to reorder commits, delete accidental commits, or compress ('Squash') multiple rapid, messy commits into one cohesive block natively
It automatically deploys the code via SSH to production
It allows connecting multiple external remote origins natively
questions.answer: It temporarily halts the timeline and opens an interactive script allowing the developer to alter history natively; enabling them to reorder commits, delete accidental commits, or compress ('Squash') multiple rapid, messy commits into one cohesive block natively
questions.explanation: If you made 7 commits titled 'wip', 'fix typo', 'fix syntax', the project history is permanently noisy. Interactive rebasing 'Squashes' those 7 micro-commits into 1 pristine, professional commit block.

questions.question: What is considered the absolute 'Golden Rule' of Git Rebasing that, if broken, will categorically destroy a team's workflow?
options: Never rebase during a weekend natively
Never interactively squash more than 3 commits simultaneously
Never rebase commits that exist on a public, shared branch (like `main`) that other developers have already pulled to their laptops locally
Never rebase when your laptop battery is physically low
questions.answer: Never rebase commits that exist on a public, shared branch (like `main`) that other developers have already pulled to their laptops locally
questions.explanation: Because rebasing mathematically destroys old commits and replaces them with new cryptographic hashes, if you rebase a shared branch, Developer B's laptop will aggressively conflict because their local history no longer aligns with the server timeline.

questions.question: If you successfully rewrite your timeline using a local interactive rebase, but GitHub rejects your subsequent `git push` because the timelines diverged natively, how must you bypass the block?
options: By deleting your GitHub account temporarily

By utilizing the highly aggressive `git push --force` command, instructing the GitHub server to mathematically overwrite and replace its remote timeline perfectly with your newly modified local timeline
By running `npm rebuild` natively
By manually merging the branch directly on the GitHub UI
questions.answer: By utilizing the highly aggressive `git push --force` command, instructing the GitHub server to mathematically overwrite and replace its remote timeline perfectly with your newly modified local timeline
questions.explanation: GitHub protects history natively. If the cryptographic hashes don't align, it blocks the push. A force push bypasses this safety lock, which is perfectly safe for a private feature branch, but catastrophic if run against `main`.

questions.question: During a rebase, if a merge conflict mathematically occurs on the 2nd commit of your replayed history, what must you do after manually editing the text file to resolve it?
options: Execute `git commit -m "Resolved"` native
Delete the repository entirely and clone again locally
Stage the resolved file using `git add`, and then execute `git rebase --continue` to instruct the algorithm to finish mathematically replaying the remaining 3 commits natively
Run `git pull` from the main branch natively
questions.answer: Stage the resolved file using `git add`, and then execute `git rebase --continue` to instruct the algorithm to finish mathematically replaying the remaining 3 commits natively
questions.explanation: Unlike standard merging which creates a single commit, rebase pauses the timeline replay exactly where the conflict occurred. Once you fix it, `--continue` re-starts the chronological mathematical replay operation.
